expectations.text: Output includes standard .kql comment header block
expectations.evidence: File has a two-line comment header ('// Brute Force Detection with Successful Authentication — Converted from Splunk SPL to KQL', '// Source: index=auth sourcetype=linux_secure...', '// Sentinel table: Syslog...') but NOT the structured header block with labeled Title, Service, Tables, Description, Parameters, and Complexity fields.

expectations.text: Uses Syslog table for Linux authentication logs
expectations.evidence: Both FailedAttempts and SuccessfulLogins subqueries use 'Syslog' table with 'Facility == "auth" or ProcessName in ("sshd", "su", "sudo")'

expectations.text: Maps Splunk dc() to KQL dcount()
Maps Splunk values() to KQL make_set()
Uses join to correlate failed logins with successful logins by user
Uses iff() or case() instead of Splunk eval/if syntax
expectations.evidence: 'success_after_bruteforce = iif(last_success >= ago(1h), "YES", "NO")' — uses iif() which is an alias for iff() in KQL
'| where TimeGenerated >= ago(LookbackWindow)' in both FailedAttempts and SuccessfulLogins

claims.claim: Uses hardcoded ago(1h) for the success_after_bruteforce check instead of a parameter
claims.evidence: 'success_after_bruteforce = iif(last_success >= ago(1h), ...)' — the 1h is hardcoded, not parameterized, unlike the with_skill output which uses 'let successWindow = 1h'

suggestions.reason: The without_skill output uses LookbackWindow = 1h (only 1 hour lookback) whereas with_skill uses 24h. The short lookback is functionally limited for brute force detection, but no assertion checks the lookback duration adequacy. Consider asserting 'Uses lookback of at least 1 hour for failed attempts'.

eval_feedback.overall: Header assertion is the key differentiator. The without_skill output uses only 1h lookback for failures, which may miss attacks — a quality gap no assertion currently catches.
